title: practical english usage - Part 297
content:
When I've recorded your voice I'll play it back.

- That was lovely. Can you play it again?

With a verb, again usually suggests repetition. Compare: 2  again      with    a  verb the shop again.) not I'm taking this meat back to the shop, (     Pm-taking this meat to not Give me my watch back, (     Give me my watch again/) normally used in this way with a verb.

in the opposite direction to an earlier movement, and similar ideas. Again is not With a verb, we  use back to suggest a return to an earlier situation, a movement 1  back      with    a  verb some differences.

Back and again can be used with similar meanings, but there are 390 back      (adverb)     and   again Everyone was asleep when I got home.

Is the baby awake yet?

predicative position (after be) than the verb forms waking and sleeping.

In informal British English the adjectives awake and asleep are more common in 2  awake        and   asleep       (adjectives) The sound of the church clock awakened a half-buried memory.

At first I paid little attention, but slowly my interest awoke.

vocabulary •         bath and bathe Let's go for a swim in the river.

swimming or just swim.

This use is rather formal; people more often say have a swim, go for a swim, go NO  BATHING   FROM   THIS   BEACH And bathe can be used to mean 'swim for pleasure’  in British English.

Your eyes are very red - you ought to bathe them.

putting water on a part of the body that hurts (for instance sore eyes).

Bathe can also be used (in both British and American English) to talk about or I always bathe before I go to bed. (AmE) (   ... take a bath) It's your turn to bathe the baby. (AmE) Bathe is the American equivalent of the British verb bath.

Pronunciation:         bathe /beɪð/      bathing /'beɪðɪŋ/       bathed /beɪðd/ 2  bathe I'm feeling hot and sticky; I think I'll have a bath.

also common in American English).

This use is rather formal; people more often say have/take a bath (and this is I don't think he baths very often.

And it can be used to mean 'bath oneself.

It's your turn to bath the baby.

It can have an object.

This verb is not used in American English.

Pronunciation:         bath /bɑ:θ/      bathing /'bɑ:θiŋ/     bathed /bu:θt/ 1  bath 391 bath     and   bathe For other uses of back and again, see a good dictionary.

Count the money again, please, (     Count again the money...) or Take back your money -1 don't want it. (   Take your money back...) object, unless this is a pronoun (►    12.4). Again cannot.

Back is an adverb particle (►   195), and can usually go between a verb and its 6 word    order 7 haven't got time to talk now.' ‘ OK,  I'll ring/call back later.' (= ring again) ‘ She's not here Just now.' ‘ Ask her to ring/call me back.' (= return my  call) both 'return a phone call' and 'repeat a phone call’ .

Note that ring back (British English only) and call back can be used to mean 5 ring/call        back I'll be (back) in the office (again) on Monday.

Go (back) to sleep (again).

He tasted the apple and spat it (back) out (again).

I stood up, and then I sat (back) down (again).

For   the   differences         between       because, as, since and    for,    377.

me for money.

Just because I'm your brother (it) doesn't mean you can keep asking Just because you're older than me (it) doesn't mean you can do what you like.

This     is    quite         a   common               structure               in    informal              speech.

3 just     because        ...  (it)  doesn't       mean       .,.

I don't think I'll go to the party... Because I'm feeling a bit tired.

’ Why are you crying? ’ Because Jack and I have had an argument!

hesitations.

can       sometimes                  stand          alone,         especially                 as    answers              or    after Because-dauses Because I worked fast, I finished early.
